sentences.text: Not ?] [?
Syndey. ?]
Stephanie [?
Marilyn ?] Druc.
Yes.
Yes, during the war.
Well, it was [?
Bulgatzka, ?] [?
Stephania ?] [?
Bulgatzka. ?] And later on, it was Stephanie [?
Apechen. ?] Then it was Stephania [?
Drutz, ?] written with a T-Z on the end, German way of spelling Druc, and then finally, the real one.
In Berlin.
Germany.
Born.
No.
Perhaps a week or two weeks, I don't know how long my mother was in hospital.
To a small town called Krotoszyn.
That was Province Posen, and it's German.
It was really Polish, part of Poland, but occupied by Germany.
Because my mother went for the birth of the child to a bigger town.
It was a small town, not a village.
My fa-- at the time when I've been born, my father was in the Prussian army.
And my mother was home duties.
Yes.
The parents of my father lived there.
Yes.
There have been a few families, but I don't know exactly how many.
Well, he went back to his occupation.
Oh, it was flour milling.
Well, my grandparents, my father's parents, have been grain merchants.
And that gave my father the idea of buying the grain in the vicinity of this town but milling it across a border in Poland occupied by Russia.
Because grain was cheaper in Prussia but dearer the flour in Russia.
And then he exported it to Russia proper also.
Yes.
Very.
Well, I don't remember my home in Krotoszyn, because I've been only very young baby, a few months old.
I've been born in March '18.
In November '18, the war finished, ended.
And my father opted.
They had the right of option either to live permanently in Germany or in Poland.
Since his business was in Poland, and since he was married to my mother, who was also from the occupied Polish town-- occupied by Russian-- you know, they moved to Poland.
What do you mean by status?
Citizenship or what?
Well, once they opted for Poland, they became Polish citizens.
And that also included--
My grandma.
I loved my grandma.
She came with us.
Yes.
Ida.
She was German by origin, but Polish by name, really.
No.
We got the Polish citizenship, because my father became a Polish citizen, as well the children.
At this time, all the children got-- in Poland, at least-- got the citizenship of their father.
Not from Warsaw.
Kalisz.
That was across the border from Prussia into Russia.
The first town was Kalisz.
And if you know a bit of history, Kalisz was run over and burned down twice by the Germans in first World War and then in the second one.
So they moved to Kalisz, not--
Yes.
The business of flour mill was in Kalisz.
On the outskirts of the town.
I did remain in Kalisz till I finished the high school.
I have a brother and a sister, which are twins, and then older sister.
What?
Vera Felicya is my older sister.
Roman Sylvim is one of the twins, and Eva Friderika is the twin.
It was a fairly large Hasidic Jewish community and a very small non-orthodox.
As a matter of fact, when my father wanted to buy an organ for the non-orthodox synagogue, they objected to it.
The Hasidics.
This synagogue was called the German Synagogue.
But in fact, it wasn't German at all.
The services had been conducted in Polish and a bit in Hebrew, not, nothing in German.
But because the reformed extremists, you probably know, came from Germany, this non-orthodox-- very much non-orthodox-- synagogue was called by the remainder of the Jews the German Synagogue.
Very large.
Yes.
No.
The Hasidics have been there for many, many centuries, because Kalisz is one of the oldest Polish little, small towns.
Oh, yes.
[INAUDIBLE]
Well, I do remember, yes.
We, at my home, usually for the new year, there has been a meal for the family, especially [? might. ?] And father used to go to the synagogue.
No.
None whatever.
None.
Gosh.
And was-- there was, there was a yeshiva, obviously, in Kalisz, [? and I would think there ?] for the city community.
I don't now.
I never heard the word yeshiva before, only after I came here.
The Hasidic Jews in Kalisz, they had mostly tiny, little shops in one of two streets close by each other.
And when I was in the shop was half a dozen kids hanging around there.
And the husband has been in the synagogue.
She was reading.
And playing the piano and being [? the lady. ?]
Oh yes.
I remember it very well.
Well, look, I've been what, 16 and 1/2, 17 when I left Kalisz.
A 17-year-old girl remembers.
It was a beautiful villa of 10 rooms and a garden.
Of course.
Yeah.
There was a cook, [?
Agnieszka-- ?] Agnes in English-- who believed that when she dies, she will be an angel in heaven with Jesus.
Then there was Maria.
That was the house maid for cleaning.
Then there was a nurse, a sister-- nursing sister for the two little twins.
And then there was my nanny, or relate as the adopted grandma.
And father had his chauffeur and his-- what do you call it-- coachman.
That's what.
Oh, yes.
Every school holidays, we went to-- we went abroad.
Yeah.
Switzerland, and Poland, and Czechoslovakia, in Europe-- mostly in Germany, really.
Because the rest of my father's family was in Germany.
He was the only one who opted for Poland.
Part of the family was in Berlin, and part was, was it, as it was called then, Breslau.
Today it is called Wroclaw.
Today, it's Polish again.
One of my father's cousin survived the war.
Well, my father's family, there have been five boys and a girl.
The girl, my auntie, by the name of Martha, she migrated just before the war to Turkey.
And she survived.
And all the boys have been migrating to Australia also before the war.
Some of them, not all, survived.
And my father's oldest boys stayed in Germany with, with the parents.
He didn't migrate.
And then he got married and stayed.
Came from Turek.
But lived in Kalisz.
So they lived in Kalisz as well.
Yeah.
One more sister.
There had been two sisters, Maria and Sophia.
Well, that is a sad story, because at the, in the first World War, this Maria, with her husband by the name of Brauda-- you might know here the [?
Brauderson ?] in Sydney-- they fled before the Germans into Russia.
And in Moscow, the husband of Maria, of my auntie, got sick.
He died there, but there was born a boy, Alexander.
And because he was born in Russia, in Moscow, then in the second World War, he was called to the army.
And especially being a doctor, the Russian army was after him.
But the auntie and her daughter, Julia, they got, they didn't get as far in the second World War as in the first one.
They also fled the Germans, but they fled through Ukraine.
And the Germans caught up with them.
And also, they Have not been anymore Jewish.
Because they had to-- since the husband was sick in Moscow in the first World War, and Jews--
Oh.
This one.
I put it back.
Where do you want me to start from?
Yeah, in the first--
Can you stop for a moment, because-- [INAUDIBLE].
Now, in the first World War, Jews have not been allowed to stay in Moscow.
And since her husband was sick and she had the baby boy, they had to become Christian.
And in the second World War, she again fled to German.
But this time, not toward Moscow, but towards Ukraine.
And the Germans caught up with them.
And because they have been Jewish by birth, the auntie and the daughter of her-- Maria and the daughter, Julia-- have been hanged in the square of a small town as a warning to other Jews.
But the boy, Alex, he was already taken into the Russian Army.
He survived.
He survived the war.
Well, after the war, he found us in [NON-ENGLISH].
And my adopted grandma gave him all sorts of things, because he had a wife and a baby.
But I didn't-- I hadn't been at home when he came, although I know that he survived.
I haven't had any more parents.
Grandparents?
No.
To the Polish government gymnasium.
No, I haven't been in any primary school.
It was private tuition for two years and then straight into the first year.
Straight into, into primary school.
And the primary school was government-run, is that?
No.
I just told you.
I haven't been in any primary school.
No.
Home tuition for a couple of years.
And straight to the gymnasium.
From first-- I've been nine-- to year eight, when I've been six and 1/2, 16 and 1/2, nearly 17.
My parents believed that two, the first two years can be taught at home, and especially since the home was a bilingual one.
We've been taught both languages.
German and Polish.
Of course.
There have been very few Jewish girls in the gymna-- it was an all-girl school, of course.
There have been very few Jews.
Because as you probably know, the Polish government didn't like to have in the government schools many Jews.
There was a Jewish gymnasium in Kalisz, too.
And most of the Jewish children, if they ever came to the gymnasium, they went to the Jewish gymnasium.
Because they have not gained the entrances to the government one.
No, it wasn't numerus clausus.
It was a test.
And that was the way of not letting them in.
But there always has been a few who have been so good that they had to be accepted.
There has usually been one or two in each of the classes.
Yes and no.
Not generally, but now and again, you could feel it.
Not officially.
Well, I don't know how to answer this question.
First of all, for the first education after the middle, after gymnasium-- or as you call it today, high school-- the universities.
If you came from a state school, you had a much easier entrance.
From the Jewish gymnasium, it was much harder to get into university.
That's number one.
Number two, the Jewish gymnasium taught Hebrew and usually religion and all this subject, where in the Polish gymnasium, you haven't had it.
It was anti-Semitic.
You see, the Hasidic community, they used to wear these long, black frocks and the little--
Curls or whatever you call it.
And they stood out from the rest of the [? people. ?] And especially if they did well.
Some of them, not all, some had been very, very poor.
But there have been a few successful.
And that always made for jealousy.
But there have been also quite good relationships.
I remember one of my, my uncle, [INAUDIBLE] he was a president of the town, though that was not anti-Semitic.
He was a Jew.
No.
Not at all.
He went once or twice a year to the synagogue.
That was all.
But he had some friends who would have been Jews, because Kalisz was a spot for the flour mills.
And there has been one or two-- at least two which I remember-- as a Jewish flour millers.
Yes.
Yes.
My father, first of all, had an another mill near Berlin and another one in Danzig, which is Gdansk today.
And he read all the German papers.
And I remember him saying, das [? wort ?] Hitler [? heisst ?] [? krieg-- ?] the word Hitler means war, though he knew exactly what's happening-- what was happening.
No, not really.

You see, my father lost his great fortune, stopped being the millionaire fairly early, because he could not look after the three mills in different-- oh, and there was another rice mill as well-- in the different places.
He couldn't be in all places at the same time, though he hired some people and they let him down.
And with other words, he lost his fortune.
And I've been only young, about what? 12 or 13.
And I started to work.
Apart from going to school, I've [INAUDIBLE].
And once he lost his fortune, he reverted to grain merchant, because that was where he came from, from his parents.
And as long as he was alive, he kept his family in this way.
But my father died in '38, and there have been still the twins at home, 10 years old, still young children.
And my mother, who has never worked in her life and wasn't used to this change of lifestyle, she couldn't really take it, and a few months later committed suicide in '39-- January, '39.
And there have been the two little children left.
And this, at that time, Vera was already married and in Warsaw.
And I've been in Warsaw, because I studied at the university.
And our grandmother adopted one.
That's a [INAUDIBLE].
Looked after the two kids at home.
When my parents died, then the two twins-- we wanted them to finish the school year still at the schools in Kalisz.
They went to a Jewish-- each to another Jewish family who kept them till the end of the school year.
And then we brought them over to Warsaw.
And they lived with us.
Yes.
Yes.
No.
Vera's husband was the manager of the Jewish students' hostel, boys hostel.
And I've been the only girl student in the hostel.
They often thanked me.
They are 100.
She is one.
There have been 100 boys, and I've been the only girl.
Anyway, we brought over the two kids and Ida into Warsaw.
That was at the end of the school year.
And not to very long afterwards, the war broke out.
I went at, in '35.
Anthropogeography.
Yes.
Two, myself and another girl.
But my parents and my sister and her husband, they wanted me to study dentistry, because that was regarded as a good profession for the future.
And I didn't want it.
And in the [? systematology ?] academy, there was an entrance exam.
And I wrote that.
There was, among the questions, there was, why do you want to be a dentist?
And I wrote down, because my family forces me.
And on the board later was the result of the exams.
There was, next to my name, a sentence that say, don't support forced labor.
And--
And I got my way.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
Very much.
Very, very much.
There has been a ripe anti-Semitism at the university.
First of all, our indexes-- that is, books where all the professors had to sign that you made it-- have been stamped in different colors.
Green, green was for the non-Jews.
And lilac stamps have been for the Jewish students, so that if you gave your index to the teacher or to the professor to--
Yes.
He immediately knew that you were a Jew.
Not my results, but in many cases, it did.
Oh, yes.
I've seen girls thrown out of the window from the first floor.
It was early '39.
I also have been during the so-called student strike, where the Jewish students have striked and didn't go to university at all.
They stayed in this Jewish hostel.
Yes.
And Jewish businesses, like bakeries and butchers, they sent into the house-- into this academic house, into the hostel-- food for the striking students.
Nothing.
Not really.
No, not really.
Vera's husband was a revisionist and very active.
But I haven't.
Not really.
You see, I've been a full-time student.
And I worked at the same time as a teller in a bank, because there have been two shifts.
The late evening/night shift, I've been a teller.
And at the same time, I have been studying full-time.
And being Jewish on top of it, you had to work really hard.
So there was not much time.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes, because being in this Jewish hostel with the students-- you know what it is with students, as always.
Well, it was quite evident that a war was coming.
I remember a demonstration in front of the English embassy in Warsaw when they declared that they support-- they come to aid of Poland.
There was a big demonstration in front of the English embassy.
For, because England has-- you know, they've been supporting the Poles against the Germans.
They declared war to the Germans.
